task: My operator handed me a plain-English ask and I genuinely don't know whether it's a quick one-shot or something that needs a real durable workflow. Which built-in workflow in the .smithers pack will classify the ask for me and then either just do it or recommend the best-fit durable workflow to run instead?
notes: route-task is the concierge entry point: classify stage decides task mode + durable, then execute (non-durable) or recommend (durable).

task: route-task's durable path ends in a 'recommend' step that points me at one seeded workflow to run. A teammate worries the agent could invent a workflow id that doesn't exist (like `mega-build`). How does route-task prevent a hallucinated workflow id from flowing downstream to the operator?
notes: A correct answer must say the recommendation is schema-enforced/constrained to the fixed set of real seeded workflow ids (implement, research-plan-implement, review, plan, research, grill-me, ralph, debug, audit, create-workflow, create-skill, extract-skill, context-doctor, monitor-smithers, triage-run, report-slideshow, eval-author, improve-test-coverage), so a hallucinated id fails validation instead of being returned. It should NOT claim there is a free-text id or a runtime existence check after the fact.

task: I have a fuzzy ask ('clean up the flaky e2e tests and make CI green again') and I want Smithers to do the context engineering for me: draft an explicit context contract from the real repo/tools/skills, grill me on the blockers, route the work, add backpressure gates, then execute and write an HTML report. Which built-in workflow is that?
notes: context-engineer is the flagship concierge proxy: classify-script, inventory-context (drafts the contract), grill, route, build-backpressure, approve-contract, execute, report.

task: I launched `context-engineer` detached and `smithers ps` shows it paused. It built a context contract and is waiting for my sign-off before it executes anything. What is the exact command to clear that gate for run cx-9f2 so the run continues?
notes: context-engineer pauses at the approve-contract gate by default; clear with `smithers approve cx-9f2 --node approve-contract` (or run with review:false to skip).

task: Recreate the tail of context-engineer as a minimal Smithers workflow: a single Sequence that first pauses at a durable human approval gate (the contract sign-off), and only after approval runs a Ralph-style loop whose one agent Task keeps working and re-running until it reports `done: true`. No real prompts needed, just the correct component structure that renders.
notes: approve-contract gate (<Approval>) before an <Loop until={done}> execute stage holding one agent <Task>, all inside <Sequence> under <Workflow>.

task: In context-engineer (and backpressure-plan), what does the 'backpressure' step actually do to the contract's success criteria, and why is it there instead of just trusting the executing agent to say it finished?
notes: A correct answer must explain that build-backpressure maps every success criterion to a concrete verification method and an enforcement gate (a gate matrix), so each 'done' claim has to be proven by evidence rather than an agent declaring victory and moving on. Bonus: it lets the execute loop honor the gates and surface what it could not finish instead of silently skipping it.

task: Before I hand a context contract to an agent, I want something to lint it deterministically and flag missing goals, ungated side effects, acceptance criteria with no verification, and required inputs with no source. Which built-in workflow does that?
notes: context-doctor: deterministic 'check' stage runs seven structural checks over the parsed contract, then an 'advise' agent proposes a fix per finding.

task: I ran `backpressure-plan` with a vague goal that had no testable acceptance criteria in it, expecting it to just produce an empty gate matrix I could fill in later. Instead the run failed. Is that a bug, and what is the workflow's intended behavior when the goal has no verifiable criteria?
notes: Correct answer: it is intended, not a bug. The extract-criteria stage fails the run loudly when a goal yields no verifiable acceptance criteria, rather than emitting an empty/vacuous gate matrix that would let work pass with no real backpressure. The fix is to restate the goal with concrete, verifiable criteria.

task: In a backpressure-plan gate matrix, each acceptance criterion is mapped to a gate that has a severity/enforcement level. What are the three gate types (the levels of how hard a gate enforces)?
notes: plan-gates assigns each criterion a gateType of blocking / warning / informational; every blocking criterion must map to a real verification method.

task: My operator wants to hand off a whole piece of work end-to-end ('Add SAML SSO to the admin console') and have Smithers play the operator role: size it, plan it, build it, validate it, launch it, review it, and deliver an evidence report, pausing only at the gates that matter. Which single meta-workflow is built for that?
notes: smithering is the 'replace the human operator' meta-workflow; routes the ask into trivial / complex / full-build tiers and runs the matching path.

task: A `smithering` full-build run keeps pausing at named approval gates as it moves through the pipeline. List every gate node id it can pause at so I can pre-stage the `smithers approve --node <id>` commands for my operator.
notes: full-build pauses at gate:prd (PRD), gate:eng (eng doc), gate:probes, gate:launch, and gate:delivery.

task: My `smithering` run r9 finished writing the PRD and paused for approval. I tried `smithers approve r9 --gate prd` and it errored. What is the correct command to clear the PRD approval gate?
notes: Approval is cleared with `smithers approve r9 --node gate:prd`. The flag is `--node` and the id is `gate:prd`; there is no `--gate` flag.

task: I want `smithering` to run completely unattended: every approval gate should auto-pass and every blocking product question should auto-adopt its recommended answer, never stopping for me. Which input do I set and to what value?
notes: Set review:false. With review:false the workflow runs without stopping; every gate auto-passes and each product question auto-adopts its recommendation.

task: I don't want `smithering` to auto-classify my ask as trivial or complex. I know this needs the whole spec/design/tickets/generated-implementation pipeline. Which input forces it to skip the classifier and run the full pipeline, and what value?
notes: Set route:'full-build' (route is auto | trivial | complex | full-build) to skip the classifier and run the full-build pipeline.

task: In smithering's full-build pipeline, the 'review' stage near the end isn't just one agent eyeballing the diff. What does it do differently, and how is the final verdict produced?
notes: Correct answer: the review stage runs several independent reviews from multiple agents (distinct reviewers/lenses) and then synthesizes them into one combined verdict, rather than relying on a single reviewer. Mentioning that the independent verifier (verify.json.pass) is the authoritative pre-merge signal while review findings are advisory is a plus, not required.

task: I started a `mission` run and it paused right after it produced the milestone plan, before doing any work. What is the exact command to approve the plan for run m-7 so it starts executing milestones?
notes: With requirePlanApproval (default true) the run waits at mission:approve-plan; clear it with `smithers approve m-7 --node mission:approve-plan`.

task: I want to run `mission` on 'Build import/export support' so the per-milestone feature workers run in isolated git worktrees and up to four of them run in parallel. Which two inputs do I set?
notes: Set useWorktrees:true for worktree isolation and maxConcurrency:4 for the parallel worker cap.

task: In `mission`, what happens when a milestone's validation (tests/build/smoke checks) fails? Does the whole run abort, does it skip to the next milestone, or something else?
notes: Correct answer: failed milestone validation triggers one targeted repair task and a revalidation before the run moves on; it does not abort the whole mission, and it does not blindly skip ahead. After all milestones complete a final task writes the mission report. Mission commits incrementally, so resuming continues from the last finished milestone.

task: Author a Smithers workflow that mirrors the mission pattern: it plans the work, pauses at a durable human approval gate before any execution, then for the active milestone dispatches multiple feature-worker agent Tasks in parallel, each inside its own isolated worktree, followed by an integration Task that reconciles their outputs. Use the real Smithers components so it renders with `smithers graph`.
canonicalAnswer: <Workflow><Sequence><Approval><Parallel><Worktree> with <Task> feature workers and an integrate <Task>
notes: Plan -> <Approval> (mission:approve-plan) -> <Parallel> of <Worktree>-isolated worker <Task>s -> integrate <Task>, all under <Sequence>/<Workflow>.

task: I ran `create-workflow` to scaffold a new automation. It clarified and designed the workflow, then paused before writing any files. Give me both: the command to approve the design gate for run cw-3, and the input that would make it skip that pause next time and run autonomously.
notes: create-workflow pauses at the approve-design gate; clear with `smithers approve cw-3 --node approve-design`, or pass review:false to run without the pause.

task: After `create-workflow` scaffolds the new workflow's .tsx and its .mdx prompts, there is a 'verify' stage. How does it confirm the freshly written workflow is valid before handing it back, and what happens if it doesn't pass the first time?
notes: Correct answer: the verify stage renders the new workflow with `smithers graph` (load + render WITHOUT executing it); if it fails, a fix agent edits the files and retries until it renders cleanly. It must NOT claim verification runs the workflow with `smithers up`, executes its agents, or runs a unit-test command.

task: create-workflow has a 'provision' stage between clarify and design. Besides pulling the relevant llms-*.txt docs and finding the closest examples/ template, what install action does it take so the generated workflow's worker agents actually have the skills they need?
notes: Correct answer: provision installs the worker skills the new workflow needs via `smithers skills add <name>` (bunx smithers-orchestrator skills add). It should reference installing/adding skills, not just reading docs.

task: Write a minimal Smithers workflow that captures create-workflow's write-gating shape: a Sequence of a 'clarify' agent Task, then a 'design' agent Task, then a durable human approval gate, then a 'scaffold' Task that writes files. Use the actual approval component Smithers ships, not an invented one, so it renders with `smithers graph`.
notes: Two agent <Task>s, then an <Approval> (approve-design) gate, then a scaffold <Task>, all in <Sequence> under <Workflow>. The approval component is <Approval>/<ApprovalGate>/<HumanTask>, never <Human> or <Approve>.

task: I want to capture a repeatable agent capability ('teach an agent to triage flaky tests') as a real agent-facing SKILL.md from a plain-English description, without hand-writing the skill structure. Which built-in workflow authors it, and which directory does it scaffold the file into?
notes: create-skill clarifies, designs, and (after the approve-design gate) scaffolds SKILL.md under .smithers/skills/.

task: I want to diagnose a stuck run with id R123 using the `triage-run` workflow. I tried `workflow run triage-run --input '{"runId":"R123"}'` and it didn't actually target R123. What is the correct input field name for the run I want triaged?
notes: The engine reserves input.runId for a run's own id, so triage-run (and monitor / report-slideshow / extract-skill) take the run to inspect as targetRunId.

task: triage-run's final 'recommend' step proposes exactly one next action plus the exact command to run. What is the closed set of actions it can recommend, and which action does it ALWAYS pick when the run is suspended on a pending approval?
notes: Correct answer: the four actions are fix, rewind, retry, and escalate. A run waiting on an approval is always escalated, never rewound or retried (a pending approval is a stuck state, not a failure). It does not mutate the run; every stage is read-only.

task: I want `smithers monitor` to not just watch run R but also apply the smallest safe self-fix and resume it, yet I don't want it touching the run until I personally OK the repair. Which flag turns on the fix, what gates it before the fix is applied, and is monitor read-only otherwise?
notes: Correct answer: pass --autofix to let it apply the smallest safe fix and resume; with requireApproval (default true) it pauses at an approve-fix <Approval> gate that you clear with `smithers approve` before any fix. With autofix off, monitor is entirely read-only (it only observes, diagnoses, and reports). The fix never approves gates, answers questions, cancels, or takes outward actions on a human's behalf.

task: The `monitor` workflow's diagnose step answers the standing questions an operator always asks about a run, from real evidence. Name those operator questions, and tell me the read-only CLI command I'd run myself to see what code a specific task changed.
notes: Correct answer must list the standing questions: what was each question and its answer (HumanTask/ask-human/Signal), what did each approval gate decide, what did each task output, and what code did each task change. The command to see a task's code diff is `smithers diff RUN_ID NODE_ID`. (output is read with `smithers output RUN_ID NODE_ID`.) Diagnosis is read-only.

task: `monitor-smithers` polls `smithers ps`, then a classify step sorts every in-flight run into a health bucket. What are the bucket names it sorts runs into?
notes: classify sorts each run into healthy / stuck / blocked / failed / overBudget; triage only runs when a non-healthy run exists.

task: `monitor-smithers` swept my fleet and flagged a couple of stuck/failed runs in its triage digest. For one of those individual stuck runs, which dedicated single-run workflow does monitor-smithers recommend I run next to diagnose it and get a concrete fix command?
notes: For stuck/failed runs the triage step recommends the triage-run workflow; for blocked runs it surfaces the pending approval/question (why/approve).

task: I have plain-English acceptance criteria ('Release notes must list every breaking change') and want a repeatable, regression-safe eval suite (JSONL cases + rubric) wired to `smithers eval`. Which built-in workflow turns the criteria into the fixture, and which directory does it write the .jsonl into?
notes: eval-author derives the suite then writes .smithers/evals/<suiteName>.jsonl and reports the exact `smithers eval` run command.

task: I want a per-group gap review of test coverage across my codebase's features, but I have no feature inventory yet. What two built-in workflows do I run, and in what order?
notes: Run feature-enum first to scan the repo into grouped SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE feature names, then audit to do the per-group gap review (it consumes feature-enum's map).

task: I already have a feature map shaped like {"GROUP":["FEATURE_A","FEATURE_B"]} and I want a parallel group-by-group gap review (missing tests, docs, observability, error handling). Which built-in workflow consumes that map via ForEachFeature?
notes: audit runs a feature-by-feature/group-by-group review using ForEachFeature over a {GROUP:[FEATURE]} map, with a configurable focus (default 'code review').

task: A long run just finished and I want one self-contained, shareable HTML slideshow of its objective, decisions, workflow graph, artifacts, failures/retries, and remaining issues. Which built-in workflow generates it, and what is the input field for the run it should report on?
notes: report-slideshow gathers the run with `smithers inspect` then renders a dependency-free HTML slideshow; the run to report on is passed as targetRunId (not runId).

task: I just finished a run and realize I keep re-deriving the same flaky-test triage approach. Which built-in workflow harvests that one-off into a reusable skill doc / workflow sketch / memory, and which input lets its analysis read the real prior run's state?
notes: extract-skill (analyze -> propose -> scaffold-skill) harvests the pattern; pass targetRunId so the analyze stage reads the real run via inspect/events/node.

task: Requirements for a request are vague and I want an agent to interrogate them one question at a time until they're clear enough to act on, with the answers captured in the run for a later plan/tickets step. Which built-in workflow runs that interview loop, and what is its default maxIterations cap?
notes: grill-me runs an interview loop via .smithers/scripts/ask-user.ts, accumulating answers in run context; maxIterations defaults to 30.

task: Author a minimal Smithers workflow that models the grill-all-three interview pattern: a single Loop, capped by maxIterations, whose one agent Task asks the user a question each iteration and stops when a `resolved` flag flips true. Keep it to the real control-flow components so it renders with `smithers graph`.
notes: One <Loop until={resolved} maxIterations=...> wrapping a single grill <Task>, inside <Sequence>/<Workflow>.

task: The `hello` starter workflow greets whoever I name, but I want it to return a haiku instead. Its behavior is a Markdown prompt, not code. Exactly which file do I edit to change what `hello` does?
notes: hello runs one agent <Task> whose prompt is .smithers/prompts/hello.mdx with {props.name} interpolated; edit that .mdx to change behavior, no code change needed.

task: The `implement`, `debug`, and `improve-test-coverage` workflows all share the same implement/validate/review loop component. What is that shared component called, and how many iterations does the loop run by default before it stops?
notes: They share the ValidationLoop component; it runs up to 3 iterations (implement stops once validation passes and a reviewer approves; debug runs all 3).

task: Both `debug` and `implement` use the same ValidationLoop with the same 3-iteration cap, yet `implement` often stops after the first green check while `debug` always runs all three iterations. Why does debug not stop early?
notes: Correct answer: implement passes early-exit (done) and prior-attempt feedback into the loop, so it can stop as soon as validation passes and at least one reviewer approves. debug deliberately does NOT pass the early-exit done signal (nor prior-attempt feedback), so it runs all iterations (up to 3) and returns the last attempt instead of bailing at the first pass.

task: The request is clear enough to lay out, but I do NOT want any code edits yet, just an ordered, reviewable implementation plan I can hand to `implement` afterward. Which read-only built-in workflow produces that?
notes: plan turns a request into a structured, ordered implementation plan without changing the repo; use it before implement/research-plan-implement/mission/tickets-create.

task: The task touches unfamiliar code and I want a tool-capable agent to inspect the repo and gather file references and prior art before I commit to a plan, without changing anything. Which read-only built-in workflow is that?
notes: research has a tool-capable agent gather repo + external context (read-only) before planning/implementation; follow with plan or mission.

task: I have a broad request ('Build the billing admin workflow') and want it split into several implementable tickets, then each ticket built in its own parallel worktree branch. Name the two workflows in order, and the directory I must drop the ticket Markdown into between them.
notes: Run tickets-create to decompose the request, copy the ticket Markdown into .smithers/tickets/, then run kanban to build each in its own worktree branch and merge on success.

task: After `kanban` runs each ticket in its own worktree branch, what decides whether a given branch actually gets merged back into the main workspace, and what happens to branches that don't qualify?
notes: Correct answer: a branch is merged only when its ValidationLoop validation passed AND at least one reviewer approved. Branches that fail validation (or get no reviewer approval) are left in their worktree branches for inspection rather than merged.

task: My request is already one narrow implementable unit ('Add a settings page for API keys') and I just want a single structured ticket (title, description, acceptance criteria), not a whole decomposition. Which built-in workflow produces one ticket?
notes: ticket-create turns a prompt into a single ticket; tickets-create (plural) is for splitting a broad request into many.

task: I want coding agents to discover and operate my repo's custom Smithers workflows without reading each .tsx source. What is the deterministic, no-agent CLI command that generates agent-facing skill docs (with each workflow's real input schema) under .smithers/skills/?
notes: `smithers workflow skills` deterministically writes skill docs under .smithers/skills/; the workflow-skill workflow is the agent-driven variant.

task: The `sweep` workflow fans a ForEachFeature review across a fixed set of cross-cutting quality topics (one prompt per topic). What are the topic dimensions it sweeps?
notes: Correct answer names the six fixed sweep topics: Documentation, E2E Testing, Unit Tests, Observability, Implementation (quality), and CLI. (Topic keys: DOCUMENTATION, E2E_TESTING, UNIT_TESTS, OBSERVABILITY, IMPLEMENTATION, CLI.) Listing all six is required; the exact casing is not.

task: The `smoketest` workflow proves a never-touched-it user can install the published smithers-orchestrator package and that every changelog claim for the version holds. Where does it get the version and the changelog under test, and why must it run in a fresh temp directory with caches cleared?
notes: Correct answer: the version is pinned by the workflow from the monorepo's package.json at load time (the agent must not guess/re-derive it); the changelog is the embedded docs/changelogs/<version>.mdx text, and each Fixes/Features bullet is a test case. It runs in a brand-new mktemp scratch dir outside the monorepo with bun/bunx caches cleared so it tests the actual published registry artifact, not a stale local/cached copy.
